coredata.dc:title: Aspect ratio's critical role in enhancing natural convective heat transfer with temperature-dependent nanofluids within rectangular enclosures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: El hadoui, Bilal
Kaddiri, Mourad
coredata.dc:description: Significant research has been performed on natural convection involving nanofluids inside rectangular enclosures since they are extensively used in thermal engineering. However, a significant gap remains in comprehending the conflicting results in nanofluid behavior observed between experimental and numerical studies. This study seeks to bridge this divide by examining natural convective heat transfer within vertical and horizontal enclosures filled with Al2O3/Water nanofluid. Using temperature-dependent viscosity and thermal conductivity, we aim to elucidate the key parameters influencing nanofluid enhancement. The results obtained through numerical simulations, using the finite difference method, showed the impact of different parameters namely nanoparticle volume fraction, 0 ≤ φ ≤ 0.05, nanoparticle size, dnp = 13, 29, and 45 nm, and aspect ratio, 0.25 ≤ A ≤ 4, on various factors, including heat transfer rates, maximal stream function, and temperature and stream function contours. The outcomes revealed that the use of nanofluids leads to improvements in heat transfer, but only when the aspect ratio is below certain critical values, Acr = 0.56, 0.48, and 0.53 for φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. Beyond these values, pure water performs better than the nanofluids in terms of heat transfer where the enhancement drops to -8%, -23%, and -22% when φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. Additionally, this critical value relies on the nanoparticle volume fraction and it rises as the nanoparticle diameter augments. This effect is believed to be caused by the high enhancement in viscosity compared to thermal conductivity when the convective mode dominates the conductive one. Furthermore, a larger aspect ratio leads to better heat transfer and the smaller nanoparticles are favorable for better heat transfer for a particular nanoparticle volume fraction. These findings carry significant implications for the field of nanofluid engineering, emphasizing the indispensable consideration of aspect ratios in optimizing thermal performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural convection
Heat transfer enhancement
Nanofluid
Temperature-dependent properties
Rectangular enclosure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of the aspect ratio and nanoparticles volume fraction
Effects of the aspect ratio and nanoparticle diameter

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical model of convection in the enclosure.
Streamlines and isotherms for dnp = 13 nm and distinct φ and A.
Variations of the average Nusselt number and central stream function with A and φ for dnp = 13 nm.
Local Nusselt number for different aspect ratios using clear water and nanofluid φ = 0.05 and dnp = 13 nm.
Temperature in the center of the left sidewall vs the aspect ratio for different nanoparticle volume fractions.
Ratio of the heat transfer vs the aspect ratio for distinct nanoparticles volume fractions and dnp = 13 nm.
Ratio of the heat transfer vs φ and distinct aspect ratios and dnp = 13 nm.
Streamlines and isotherms for φ = 0.05 and different nanoparticles diameter and A.
Variations of the ratio of the average Nusselt number and central stream function with A and different dnp for φ = 0.01.
Physical properties of the used nanoparticles and water [26].
Comparison of nanofluid natural convection results from [55] for A = 8.
Comparison of nanofluid natural convection results from [57] for Pr = 6.2 and Ra = 105.
Comparison of nanofluid natural convection results with experimental [8] and numerical ones [56].
Convergence tests for different A and φ = 0.05, dnp = 13 nm, Pr = 6.5, and Ra = 104.

head.ce:title: Aspect ratio's critical role in enhancing natural convective heat transfer with temperature-dependent nanofluids within rectangular enclosures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences and Technologies, Laboratory of Industrial Engineering and Surface Engineering, Sultan Moulay Slimane University, B.P. 523, Beni-Mellal 23000, Morocco

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Sciences and Technologies, Laboratory of Industrial Engineering and Surface Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences and Technologies, Laboratory of Industrial Engineering and Surface Engineering, Sultan Moulay Slimane University, B.P. 523, Beni-Mellal 23000, Morocco

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Significant research has been performed on natural convection involving nanofluids inside rectangular enclosures since they are extensively used in thermal engineering. However, a significant gap remains in comprehending the conflicting results in nanofluid behavior observed between experimental and numerical studies. This study seeks to bridge this divide by examining natural convective heat transfer within vertical and horizontal enclosures filled with Al2O3/Water nanofluid. Using temperature-dependent viscosity and thermal conductivity, we aim to elucidate the key parameters influencing nanofluid enhancement. The results obtained through numerical simulations, using the finite difference method, showed the impact of different parameters namely nanoparticle volume fraction, 0 ≤ φ ≤ 0.05, nanoparticle size, dnp = 13, 29, and 45 nm, and aspect ratio, 0.25 ≤ A ≤ 4, on various factors, including heat transfer rates, maximal stream function, and temperature and stream function contours. The outcomes revealed that the use of nanofluids leads to improvements in heat transfer, but only when the aspect ratio is below certain critical values, Acr = 0.56, 0.48, and 0.53 for φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. Beyond these values, pure water performs better than the nanofluids in terms of heat transfer where the enhancement drops to -8%, -23%, and -22% when φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. Additionally, this critical value relies on the nanoparticle volume fraction and it rises as the nanoparticle diameter augments. This effect is believed to be caused by the high enhancement in viscosity compared to thermal conductivity when the convective mode dominates the conductive one. Furthermore, a larger aspect ratio leads to better heat transfer and the smaller nanoparticles are favorable for better heat transfer for a particular nanoparticle volume fraction. These findings carry significant implications for the field of nanofluid engineering, emphasizing the indispensable consideration of aspect ratios in optimizing thermal performance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the major importance of the phenomenon of natural convection in enclosures, researchers have put a lot of work into that because it can be present in many industrial applications such as combined-cycle power plants [1], heating of buildings, cooling systems [2], heat exchangers [3], and solar collectors [4]. Therefore, several experimental studies on natural convection in different configurations, where the square enclosure has received sufficient attention from the scientific community, can be found in [5–12]. In these experimental studies, enclosures were filled with different fluids and heated from different sidewalls. However, numerical methods offer effort and time efficiency over experimental ones when investigating natural convection in cavities and they provide an accurate control of variables and better visualization of the complicated flow patterns. These advantages made them suitable for extensive parametric analysis and an excellent method for gaining valuable insight into scientific research and engineering design. Therefore, other studies have investigated the natural convective flow in different situations of boundary conditions, using different numerical methods [13].

Thermal oils and water have been widely used in thermal applications over the past three decades. However, the demand for more reliable heat transfer fluids brought about the creation of nanofluids; defined as base fluids in which nanoscale metallic and non-metallic particles are suspended. This type of fluids is characterized by minor clogging, sedimentation, and pressure drop and higher thermal conductivity resulting in higher heat transfer efficacy and stability. Given these attributes, nanofluids have had the potential to be utilized across a variety of engineering applications, including cooling and heating of buildings [14], refrigerators, heat exchangers [15], solar heaters, heat storage [16], nuclear reactors [17], heat sinks [18] and car radiators [19]. Furthermore, Saidur et al. [20] have conducted a thorough examination of the literature regarding the applications and difficulties associated with nanofluids. Habibishandiz et al. [21] scrutinized through a numerical software natural convective flow of nanofluids with oxytactic microorganisms inside a square porous enclosure where they intended to apply periodic thermal boundary conditions, instead of constant temperature, on the sidewalls to eliminate the microorganisms’ negative impact on the mean Nusselt number. Krishna et al. [22] studied the heat transfer in TiO2/H2O and Al2O3/H2O nanofluids inside a rectangular microchannel for cooling electronic equipment and proved that the thermal transfer rates improved up to 59% and 38% respectively for both nanofluids at 2.5% of volume fraction. Alhajaj et al. [23] compared different configurations with different nanofluids in order to find the best setup for better heat extraction from hot surfaces. They found that for a higher efficiency index (lower pressure drop and higher Nusselt number) nanofluid of 0.5% volume fraction and alumina nanoparticles in water base must be used. Hasan et al. [24] used four nanoparticles namely CuO, SiO2, Al2O3, and ZnO to improve the heat transfer rate in different helical heat exchangers and it was proved that Al2O3 offered a higher rate while SiO2 provided the lowest. Mustafa et al. [25] analyzed the advantage of incorporating copper oxide water-based nanofluids to increase the effectiveness of solar-assisted hot process stream in which the used nanofluid led to a better energy-saving potential compared to pure water. Hu et al. [6] investigated, by means of experimental and numerical methods, the natural convective flow of the nanofluid inside enclosures of square shapes. Their computational outcomes were in concordance with the outcomes of the experiment and enhancement in heat transfer rate relied on nanoparticles mass fractions. Abbassi et al. [26] simulated convective flow within an incinerator containing a heater and filled with nanofluids. The outcomes demonstrated that the rise in the nanosolids volume fraction, heater dimension, and Rayleigh number improves the heat transfer while the Hartman number results in a reduction, and to better reduce entropy and optimize heat transfer, the incinerator inclination must be equal to 90° or 270°. Alsabery et al. [27] scrutinized the transient natural convective flow inside trapezoidal cavities confining non-Newtonian nanofluid having a sinusoidal distribution of the temperature on the vertical sidewalls. A study carried out by Aghakhani et al. [28] focused on heat transfer by natural convection with entropy generation analysis in nanofluids inside an inclined enclosure. This latter was imposed to radiation and magnetic field, while the temperature was held constant in an elliptical manner. Qi et al. [29] investigated a 2D natural convective flow of hybrid nanofluids using lattice Boltzmann method. They considered a two-phase model and a vertical rectangular cavity with Dirichlet boundary conditions. They found that adding Cu nanoparticles to Al2O3/water nanofluids gives a higher improvement than Al2O3/water nanofluid alone. Dogonchi et al. [30] considered a wavy complex cavity with a partially hot sidewall to examine the impact of the nanoparticles’ shapes in a magneto-hydrodynamic natural convection. Hasan et al. [31] scrutinized free transient convective flow in a triangular cavity containing CNT-water nano-liquid and heated sinusoidally. They found that when the nanoparticle concentration augments, the average fluid temperature and heat transfer rate enhance and a higher heat transfer rate was obtained using the nanoliquid with 15% of volume ratio. Islam et al. [3] analyzed magnetohydrodynamic natural convective flow within a prismatic heat exchanger with copper nanosolids dispersed in water. The study has shown that increases in nanoparticle volume fraction and Rayleigh number improve heat transfer while augmenting Hartman's number reduces it. Alam et al. [32] examined numerically transient magnetohydrodynamic free convective nanofluid flow inside a semicircular cavity. The outcomes showed that the rise in Rayleigh number leads to a more rapid attainment of the stable solution. Nanofluids, even at low nano-solid volume fractions, have shown better average thermal transfer rates compared to base fluids. Among the different nanofluids, Co/kerosene had the highest Nusselt number. Oztop and Abu-Nada [33] revealed that the improvement in heat transfer when utilizing nanofluids is more noticeable in cases with low aspect ratios compared with high ones. The exact same results were obtained in [34]. Wang et al. [35] examined how temperature-dependent properties affect the natural convective flow of Al2O3/water nanofluids in rectangular cavities with a sinusoidal temperature pattern. The results indicated an optimal nanoparticle concentration that maximizes heat transfer improvement, with this optimum concentration increasing as the temperature and temperature difference rise. Furthermore, the average Nusselt number decreases as the power-law index increases and there's an optimal aspect ratio that has the most substantial impact on heat transfer. Similarly, Abu-Nada et al. [36] studied the effect of nanofluid variable properties on natural convection in enclosures with constant temperature on the vertical walls. They showed that with enclosures of high aspect ratio there is significant deterioration in average Nusselt numbers compared with enclosures with low aspect ratio.

So far, researchers have been proposing many models for both the effective viscosity and thermal conductivity of the nanofluids. The earliest model estimating the effective viscosity is the well-used Brinkman's model [37] which is a revised form of the model of Einstein. As Brinkman's model can be used for a reasonable range of nanoparticle concentrations, it has been adopted in loads of studies. Moreover, the development of techniques measuring thermal conductivity encouraged experimenters to develop different expressions such as Chon's model [38]. The main factors, in addition to the base fluid and nanoparticles concentration and nature, that affect these physical properties of nanofluids are the Brownian motion, thermophoresis, nano-layering interfaces, nanoparticle sizes and shapes, and clustering of nanoparticles. The readers are referred back to the review articles [39,40] for more details. Moreover, the employed models in the numerical investigations were proved to have an essential impact on the behavior of the nanofluids [36,41].

Surprisingly, there is a big discrepancy between the outcomes of the experimental and numerical studies that treat nanofluid behavior in natural convection. Chang et al. [42] studied experimentally free convective flow of Al2O3/H2O nanofluids inside thin enclosures, according to their study, a decrease in Nusselt number was observed in comparison with clear water. This decrease became more prominent for smaller Rayleigh numbers and higher nanoparticle concentrations; and was believed to be caused by sedimentation. Li's experimental study [43] showed as well that the natural convective coefficient deteriorated when nanoparticle concentration in the base fluid increased. Hu et al.’s study [7] yielded a similar outcome, wherein the nanofluid convective heat transfer for low Rayleigh numbers was found to be no better than that of the base fluid and may even be worse. Furthermore, the study found that the TiO2/water nanofluid natural convective heat transfer was further influenced by viscosity compared to thermal conductivity. Another behavior of nanofluid is that other parameters can influence the effect of adding nanoparticles changing it from being useful to heat transfer to being disadvantageous. Hu's experimental study [6] discovered that nanofluid heat transfer exhibited an improvement compared to clear water at lower nanoparticle mass fractions. However, at higher nanoparticle mass fractions, a reduction of heat transfer was observed. A numerical and experimental analysis of the nanofluid MHD free convective flow inside a cubic enclosure was carried out by Kargarsharifabad [44]. In this work, the enclosure was subjected to both Dirichlet and Neumann conditions on the vertical walls and filled with copper/water nanofluid. He proved that there is a threshold value of the Hartman number that alters the thermal enhancement of the nanofluid into deterioration because of the enhancement in the electrical conductivity. Heris et al. [45] experimentally carried out a study on natural convective flow and revealed that augmenting the nanoparticles concentration may result in degradations of thermal transfer rate. Ho et al. [8] evidenced that when the Al2O3 volume fraction is above 2%, the mean Nusselt number is smaller than that of the base fluid. Ali et al. [46] conducted an experiment on the free convective flow of alumina water-based nanofluids within a circular vertical cavity where the bottom boundary was exposed to uniform heat flux. The outcomes revealed the existence of an optimal nanofluid concentration. Liao [47] addressed an interesting study of nanofluid natural convective flow in a differentially heated cavity. This study focused mainly on the heat transfer transitions between three types of heat transfer nanofluid modes namely the continuous enhancement with nanoparticle concentration, the existence of the optimum nanoparticles’ concentration, and the invariant mode with the nanoparticles’ concentration. This transition was controlled by the thermal Rayleigh number which was itself dependent on average temperature, nanoparticle diameter, and the temperature difference. Alsabery et al. [48] proved that using the model of Buongiorno, an optimal average volume fraction was found at high Rayleigh numbers. On the contrary, with the augmentation in nanosolids volume fraction and Rayleigh number, using the homogeneous model resulted in a rise in the heat transfer rate. Manaa et al. [49] found that the overall Nusselt number declines as the nanosolids concentration augments. Cicek et al. [50] explored the hybrid nanofluid's deposition and migration in a square enclosure. They have found that the growth in volume fraction leads to a significant increase of mean Nusselt number if Rayleigh numbers are low, while regarding the strength of flow structure and heat transfer enhancement the optimum volume fraction is 0.1% for Rayleigh numbers above 104. Bondareva et al. [51] investigated natural convective flow inside an open cavity. In their study they used experimental correlations to approximate the effective viscosity and thermal conductivity of Al2O3/H2O nanofluids, they found that the addition of nanoparticles volume fraction brings about a smaller heat transfer rate compared with the base fluid. Santra et al. [52] investigated heat transfer in a square enclosure containing non-Newtonian Cu/water nanofluid. With increasing the volume fraction of nanoparticles, it has been found that there is a decrease in heat transfer.

Most previous studies on natural convective flows of nanofluids have focused on temperature-imposed boundary conditions or have used simple models of nanofluids and a fixed aspect ratio, often with a square enclosure, because of that they obtained a better enhancement in heat transfer compared to base fluids. However, as established by the aforementioned literature survey, additional investigations are required in order to identify the role of other pertinent parameters such as the nanoparticle volume fraction, aspect ratio, and nanoparticle diameter and to explore in-depth nanofluid behavior resulting in either enhanced or deteriorated heat transfer. Thus, the present study aims to investigate the impact of the aspect ratio, nanoparticle size, and volume fraction of Al2O3 nanoparticles suspended in clear water on the fluid flow and heat transfer in rectangular cavities with uniform heat fluxes on the sidewalls, which is more representative of real-world applications like cooling electronic components. To date, no study has explored this concept, and our findings are unique and innovative and the important motivation is that the outcomes of this study can be helpful for engineering applications.

Fig. 1 . shows the geometry that is being investigated in this paper, a two-dimensional rectangular enclosure of width W and height H. Horizontal walls are thermally insulated, while vertical sidewalls are subjected to uniform heat flux, q; all walls are immobile. The cavity is filled with a mixture of Al2O3 nanoparticles suspended in water.

The nanofluid is considered to be a single-phase and incompressible fluid and the flow is laminar. The properties of the nanofluid are dependent on the size and volume fraction of the nanoparticles. The nanoparticles and base fluid are in thermal equilibrium. The density of the nanofluid in the buoyancy force term follows the Boussinesq approximation ρnf(T) = ρnf,0(1 - βnf(T - T0) ) where T0 = 22°C is the reference temperature, while the viscosity and thermal conductivity are estimated using thermo-independent models. the thermophysical properties of water and nanoparticles are shown in Table 1 .

The governing equations, considering the assumptions above, describing the conservation of mass (1), momentum (2) and (3), energy (4), and their physical boundary conditions (5) and (6) are given as follows:

To avoid pressure gradients in the momentum Eqs. (2) and (3), the stream function–vorticity formulation (Ψ - Ω) is adopted. Thus, the dimensionless steady-state equations governing the conservation of mass, momentum, and energy and their boundary conditions are as follows:

T, u, and v are respectively the dimensionless temperature, and horizontal and vertical velocities, Pr and RaT are the Prandtl and thermal Rayleigh numbers and A is the aspect ratio.

The problem of this study is tackled by using an in-house code written in FORTRAN and based on the finite difference method and the line-by-line ADI technique. The discretized equations lead to the formation of two tridiagonal matrix systems, one with implicit discretization along the Ox-axis and explicit discretization along the Oy-axis, and the other one with the opposite. The stream function equation is resolved by applying the PSOR method to satisfy the conservation of mass. The subsequent convergence condition is implemented to confirm the steady-state solution

|
where i,j denote grid location in Ox and Oy directions, respectively and f denotes temperature T and velocities u and v.

The dimensionless quantities measuring the local and average heat transfer rates on the left wall in addition to the enhancement ratio which are calculated based on the base fluid properties and the cavity width are defined as:

×
100

The accuracy of the current numerical solution was verified by comparing its results with different former research studies [8,55–57] in terms of the average Nusselt number and maximal stream function. Tables 2–4
clearly show that the results of this code are in perfect accordance with the published works where the highest relative error does not even exceed 3%, so that we can build enough confidence in the accuracy of our code.

Finding the perfect balance between the accuracy of results and computational time is so important, primarily simulations were run using different uniform grid meshes. From Table 5 , for a broad range of the cavity aspect ratio, further refinement of the 101 × 101 grid size was found to have no significant impact on the results of the average Nusselt number and the central value of the stream function.

In this section, we present the numerical results of this study using contours of streamlines and isotherms to visualize dynamic and thermal fields within the whole enclosure and also in terms of the stream function absolute intensity in the center and the average and local Nusselt number to quantitively evaluate the flow intensity and convective heat transfer rate in addition to the enhancement ratio for distinct nanoparticles diameter (dnp = 13, 29 and 45 nm), aspect ratio (0.25 ≤ A ≤ 4) and nanoparticles volume fraction (0 ≤ φ ≤ 0.05) while other parameters are kept fixed (RaT = 104, Pr = 6.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the aspect ratio and nanoparticles volume fraction
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the contours of temperature and stream function for distinct nanoparticle volume fractions and aspect ratios. The colorful pattern is for clear water, φ = 0.0, the black, white, and red lines are for nanofluids, φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. As the left sidewall is attached to the heating source, the streamlines were recorded moving in a clockwise direction, rising next to the left sidewall and descending alongside the right one due to the density variations. All the stream function values are extremum in the center of the cavity, which is why we will present the absolute values of the central stream function in the following figures. Enlarging the cavity width results in changing the streamlines’ shape to cover the whole space of the cavity. A close look at the streamlines reveals that the counters in the center of the cavity are shrinker for nanofluids compared to clear water and this shrinkage becomes significant for horizontal enclosures in comparison with the vertical ones. To explain this, looking at the Eqs. (2 and 3), the velocity fields are explicitly affected by the effective kinematic viscosity ϑr and thermal expansion coefficient βr, these physical properties vary with the nanoparticles volume fraction Eqs. (15) and (16) where ϑr increases and βr decreases with the increase in φ. This results in an augmentation in the resistance of nanofluid flow and a reduction in the momentum force respectively. On the other hand, by looking at the isotherms, it can be observed that they get more deformed as the aspect ratio increases which means that the thermal boundary layer gets thinner and the convective transfer mode becomes predominant. In contrast, the augmentation in φ results in an opposite effect which is due to the increase in the effective thermal diffusivity with φ and the nanoparticle volume fraction effect becomes more significant as the aspect ratio increases.

Fig. 3 depicts the variations of the average Nusselt number and the central stream function with A and different nanoparticle volume fractions. Firstly, the aspect ratio affects these quantities in different ways. The average Nusselt number exhibits a single flat region when A is small, but it experiences a notable rise as A increases, meaning that for low aspect ratios, the variation of Nu is independent of A but the continuous increase in A leads to a better heat transfer rate. On the contrary, the central stream function increases first and then becomes independent of A as this latter increases. For low aspect ratios, the low values of both
Nu
︸
and |Ψc| imply that conduction is the predominant mode but as this parameter increases, convection becomes the dominant mode. This is because as A increases, the nanofluid gets more space to remove heat from the heated side wall and convey it to the right one.

The most interesting remark from this figure is that the volume fraction of nanoparticles behaves differently depending on the aspect ratio. For low values of A, using nanofluids enhances heat transfer, but as we reach a critical value of the aspect ratio Acr nanofluid deteriorates heat transfer. This can be explained by the fact that the enhancement of the thermal conductivity outstands the enhancement of the viscosity in the conductive mode, in this mode thermal properties have more importance. However, when the convective mode is reached, the effect of the viscosity becomes more important and causes the velocity of the nanofluid to slow. This is proved from the stream function values which are always declined with φ.

Analyzing the local profiles of Nusselt number for various aspect ratios, when pure water and nanofluid (φ = 0.0, 0.05) are used, Fig. 4 reveals that for very small aspect ratios (A = 0.25 and 0.5) using nanofluid is beneficial for heat transfer but increasing A leads to an unfavorable effect of nanofluids. This indicates that nanofluids are only favorable when heat transfer is dominated by diffusion since the profile of Nu(y) is linear when A = 0.25 and 0.5. The profile of local Nu for clear water is different from the one of nanofluid for A = 0.5, this is because using nanofluid retards the convective mode to predominate the heat transfer.

As seen from this figure, the difference between pure water and nanofluid in Nu(y) escalates with the rise in aspect ratio firstly in the central part of the enclosure then followed by the corners. For instance, for A = 1 we notice that clear water provides better heat transfer than nanofluid in the center of the cavity but close to the corners nanofluid is comparable to clear water, this indicates that the difference in the vertical walls temperatures in this range is smaller for water than that of nanofluid. Thus, the change in the nanofluid's behavior starts in the middle and then propagates to the corners.

In order to explain this behavior, Fig. 5 presents the temperature in the middle of the hot sidewall as a function of the aspect ratio (around the critical value) for different nanoparticle volume fractions. The lower temperature inside the enclosure is an indication of transferring the heat from the hot sidewall to the cold one and results in a better heat transfer rate. As can be seen from this figure, an increase in A results in better cooling of the left sidewall (the temperature of the wall decreases). For a particular nanoparticle volume fraction and aspect ratio, the nanofluid temperature becomes hotter than that of clear water, Acr = 0.56, 0.48, and 0.53 for φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 respectively. Using nanofluid beyond these aspect ratios weakens the cooling of the hot wall compared to pure water. This behavior was noticed for the cold vertical sidewall but not presented to save space.

Fig. 6 presents the ratio of Nusselt number vs aspect ratio for distinct nanoparticle volume fractions. The enhancement of the Nusselt number can reach 6%, 14%, and 20% when φ = 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05, respectively for vertical enclosures but this enhancement decreases as the aspect ratio increases and changes into a deterioration for horizontal enclosures where it reaches -8%, -23% and -22% when φ = 0.01, 0.03 and 0.05 respectively. The existence of the critical aspect ratio is clear in this figure and it depends on the value of φ.

The conjugated effect of φ with the aspect ratio is depicted in Fig. 7 . where the ratio of the Nusselt number is plotted as a function of φ for different aspect ratios. It is shown that for all nanoparticle volume fractions, there is an improvement in heat transfer rate when aspect ratios are below 0.4. However, for an aspect ratio equal to 0.5, there is a degradation of heat transfer when using nanofluid with 0.02 of volume fraction, but beyond that volume fraction nanofluid results in an enhanced heat transfer. On the other hand, further augmentation in aspect ratio leads to a change in the behavior of nanofluids for all nanoparticle volume fractions. We should note that the viscosity of the nanofluid for φ = 0.02 is maximal leading to a maximum deterioration and it decreases with the rise of φ, this shows the big effect that the effective viscosity plays in heat transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the aspect ratio and nanoparticle diameter
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 . presents the temperature and stream function contours for distinct nanoparticle diameters and aspect ratios. The colorful pattern is for clear water, φ = 0.0, the black, red, and white lines are for nanofluids of φ = 0.05 and dnp = 13, 29, and 45 nm respectively. The impact of nanoparticle size on the stream function contours is small because the fluid flow is affected implicitly by the thermal conductivity when the nanoparticle diameter changes. However, the isotherms are explicitly affected by the change in the nanoparticle diameter for all aspect ratios where bigger nanoparticles result in thicker thermal boundary layers. Thermal diffusivity is enhanced with the decrease in nanoparticle diameter resulting in enhancement in thermal diffusion over convection.

Fig. 9 presents the effect of the nanoparticle size on the enhancement ratio of
Nu
︸
and |Ψc| for different aspect ratios. As can be seen, using small nanoparticles gives better heat transfer compared to bigger nanoparticles, especially for small A where the enhancement can reach 6% with dnp = 13 nm. It is clear from this figure that each nanoparticle size has a different critical aspect ratio. Smaller nanoparticles size can retard the negative effect of nanofluid and requiring a bigger critical aspect ratio. For the central stream function, using nanofluids always leads to a reduction in the flow intensity, however, smaller nanoparticle size gives less reduction for horizontal enclosures and more reduction for vertical enclosures.

The study focused on examining the issue of consistent natural convection heat transfer within a rectangular container containing a mixture of Al2O3 nanoparticles and water. This investigation employed Chon's and Abu-nada's models, taking into account the temperature-dependent variations in thermal conductivity and viscosity. Numerical results were obtained for different aspect ratios, nanoparticle sizes, and volume fractions in terms of central stream function, local and average Nusselt number, and enhancement ratio. The findings are as follows:
•
The outcomes of this study revealed the existence of the conjugated effect between the aspect ratio and nanoparticle volume fraction where we can obtain an enhancement in Nusselt number with the use of nanofluids only when the aspect ratio is less than a critical value and beyond that value pure water gives better heat transfer rate compared to nanofluid.
•
This critical value augments with the rise in nanoparticle size and it depends on the nanoparticle volume fraction.
•
Adding nanofluid is likely to decrease the transfer of heat depending on the aspect ratio, caused by a high increase in viscosity compared with thermal conductivity.
•
The competition of these properties is based on the predominant mode of transmission. With nanofluid, thermal conductivity enhancement is achieved compared to viscosity in conductive mode, and this results in enhanced heat transfer. In contrast, nanofluids have a negative effect on heat transfer in convective mode because of the important role that viscosity plays and its ability to overcome thermal conductivity increase.
•
For all nanoparticle volume fractions, the rise in the aspect ratio and decrease in nanoparticle size result in a better heat transfer rate.
As a prospect for future research, further investigation could focus on modeling nanofluids as non-Newtonian fluids and investigating mixed convection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Viscosity of nanofluids: a review of recent experimental studies
Int. Commun. Heat Mass Transf.

Int. J. Heat Mass Transf.

Li, “Numerical simulation of natural convection of nanofluid in a square enclosure: effects due to uncertainties of viscosity and thermal conductivity,” Int. J. Int. J. Heat Mass Transf.
